candidate_problems.text: Can the proposed 'record space' R be defined non-circularly and derived from underlying dynamics alone? The conjecture relies on stable equivalence classes, coarse-graining, and attractor-like pointer records, but it remains open whether there is a principled, observer-independent construction of R that does not smuggle in classicality, preferred macroscopic variables, or pragmatic notions of robustness. This is a key structural question because if R is not uniquely or naturally definable, the account of definiteness may be underdetermined.

Is an additional 'selector' beyond unitary evolution genuinely necessary, or can actuality be reconstructed from decoherent branch structure without adding new ontology? The conjecture asserts that bare quantum structure yields possible definite records but not one actual outcome. The major unresolved tension is whether this is a real incompleteness or an artifact of assuming single-outcome actuality. This opens a sharp problem: formulate and compare candidate selectors (collapse law, hidden variables, self-locating uncertainty, modal actualization, etc.) against the possibility that no selector is needed.

How, if at all, do the weights |c_i|^2 acquire normative or physical significance within the record-space picture? Even if decoherence yields stable sectors, the conjecture leaves open why amplitude-squared should govern realized frequencies, rational credence, or any selector dynamics. The unresolved issue is whether the Born rule can be derived from stability/invariance properties of record classes, or whether it too requires extra postulates. This is worth pursuing because any account of outcome selection is incomplete without explaining the role of the weights.
